intro: Python prioritizes rapid, scriptable workflows through dynamic runtime typing, whereas C# provides a highly structured, type-safe environment backed by the robust .NET framework. Developers migrate to C# to build highly maintainable, enterprise-grade architectures and multi-threaded enterprise services. This shift introduces rigid static compilation rules, formal interface design patterns, and an advanced, highly performant runtime engine.

diffs.from: Strong, dynamic typing. Variable definitions do not require type specifications, allowing flexibility at runtime.
diffs.to: Strong, static typing. Supports implicit var type declarations, explicit interface blueprints, generics, and strict compile-time validation.

diffs.from: Minimalist layout defined by semantic whitespace, utilizing lightweight declarations and conversational keywords.
diffs.to: Structured block syntax using braces and explicit type definitions. Highly object-oriented layout requiring explicit namespaces and class containers.

diffs.from: Memory lifecycle managed by automated reference counting combined with a secondary tracing garbage collection engine.
diffs.to: Managed by a generational, tracing garbage collector (GC) highly tuned for multi-threaded systems and object tracking optimizations.

diffs.from: Driven by an event-loop system utilizing the asyncio module ecosystem and basic cooperative task constructs.
diffs.to: First-class integration using the Task-based Asynchronous Pattern (TAP) powered natively by async/await and thread pool scaling.

diffs.from: Wide, cross-domain modules focusing heavily on scripting, file automation, and data handling workflows.
diffs.to: Deep .NET Base Class Library (BCL) providing unified, enterprise-grade capabilities for cryptography, async workflows, and networking.

examples.title: Fetching JSON data asynchronously
examples.description: Compares a Python asynchronous HTTP call to the equivalent C# Task-based HttpClient architecture.
examples.fromCode:
import asyncio
import aiohttp

async def fetch_data(url):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.get(url) as response:
            return await response.json()

examples.title: Filtering a collection with a predicate
examples.description: Contrasts a Python list comprehension with C#'s functional LINQ pipeline syntax.
examples.fromCode:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
evens = [x for x in nums if x % 2 == 0]

tips.title: Utilize LINQ for Queries
tips.body: Replace Python's list comprehensions and lambda expressions with C# Language Integrated Query (LINQ) extension methods for clean filtering.

tips.title: Leverage Built-In Properties
tips.body: Avoid writing manual getter and setter methods or using @property. C# provides auto-implemented properties (e.g., public string Name { get; set; }) natively.

tips.title: Understand Value vs Reference Types
tips.body: Be aware that primitives (int, bool, struct) are stored as value types on the stack, while classes are reference types residing on the heap.

tips.title: Configure Explicit Error Handlers
tips.body: C# relies exclusively on structured exception handling. Ensure your exceptions map to precise, type-safe catch blocks rather than broad catches.

faqs.question: What is the equivalent of a Python dict in C#?
faqs.answer: The primary equivalent is the `Dictionary<TKey, TValue>` class, located in the `System.Collections.Generic` namespace, which enforces compile-time types for keys and values.

faqs.question: Can I use dynamic variables in C# like I do in Python?
faqs.answer: Yes, C# includes the `dynamic` keyword, which bypasses compile-time type checking and resolves member invocations at runtime using the Dynamic Language Runtime (DLR).

faqs.question: How does null safety compare between the two languages?
faqs.answer: Modern C# supports Nullable Reference Types, which trigger compiler warnings if an object might be null, offering stronger safety guarantees than typical Python execution.
